title: Baby Sprinkle

jokes.text: I love your pajama set.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn answers the door looking disheveled; David asks if everything is okay.
jokes.punchline: Instead of addressing the obvious distress, David diplomatically compliments what is clearly pajama attire worn in public.
jokes.explanation: Classic David Rose diplomacy — acknowledging a disaster with a polished, backhanded compliment. High character integration because only David would sidestep obvious chaos by complimenting the wardrobe choice with total sincerity. The economy is excellent: three words do a lot of work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Have you tried lighting a patchouli candle? It centers the chakras.

jokes.text: David lit a patchouli candle to 'calm her chi'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is clearly stressed; David acknowledges the hectic energy
jokes.punchline: His solution is lighting a patchouli candle — a fussy, wellness-coded non-solution delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: A small but effective character beat — David's boutique-owner fussiness expressed through a meaningless wellness gesture. Not a big joke but consistent with his persona.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I couldn't sleep because I kept picturing the baby doing synchronized swimming in my stomach.

jokes.text: Somebody decided to do a synchronized swimming routine at 2am
jokes.setup: Jocelyn explains she didn't get much sleep last night
jokes.punchline: The reason is that her unborn baby was doing a 'synchronized swimming routine at 2am' — a vivid, absurd euphemism for fetal kicking
jokes.explanation: A genuinely well-constructed image. 'Synchronized swimming' is specific and funny — it implies coordination and showmanship from a fetus. Good originality and economy in how it reframes a common pregnancy complaint.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: My sister can't come. Her husband's having heart problems.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, well, I'm sure he'll be fine. These things have a way of working themselves out.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: He's dead, David.

jokes.text: 'Something to do with her husband's heart.' / 'Oh, well, hope it clears itself up.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn mentions her sister can't come because of her husband's heart condition
jokes.punchline: David cavalierly responds 'hope it clears itself up' as though a cardiac event is a mild inconvenience
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work. David's breezy dismissal of a heart condition as something that might 'clear itself up' perfectly encapsulates his self-absorption and social obliviousness. The follow-up 'Well, you never know' / 'Well, I do' is a tight little two-step.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A self-hosted baby shower. How... enterprising of you.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, well, it won't. That's the problem.' / 'Well, you never know.' / 'Well, I do.'
jokes.setup: David continues to offer hollow optimism about the heart condition
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn flatly shuts it down with 'Well, I do' — indicating the husband's condition is serious and David's cheeriness is absurd
jokes.explanation: A crisp back-and-forth. The rhythm of false hope → deflation is well-timed, and Jocelyn's deadpan certainty lands as a good button. Economy is the real strength here.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I'm trying to decorate for the baby shower, but nothing seems quite right.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: What about hanging up 'The Pelican Brief' poster? It's got such lovely colors!

jokes.text: 'Sounds very dark.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn announces she is forced to throw her own baby shower
jokes.punchline: David's only response is 'Sounds very dark' — an understated, mildly theatrical reaction that captures his aesthetic sensibility
jokes.explanation: Three words, perfect David voice. He treats the social humiliation of self-hosting a baby shower with the gravity of a Bergman film. Very high economy, strong character integration. A small but sharp line.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I really need someone like David to help me organize my closet.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Or you.

jokes.text: Roland offered his movie posters — specifically 'The Pelican Brief' — as baby shower decorations
jokes.setup: Jocelyn explains that Roland tried to help with decorations
jokes.punchline: He offered his movie posters, and the specific poster cited is 'The Pelican Brief' — an aggressively non-festive, obscure early-90s legal thriller
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully specific joke. 'The Pelican Brief' is funny precisely because it's so wrong — it's not even a bad movie, it's just bafflingly inappropriate. It perfectly characterizes Roland's oblivious good intentions. The specificity elevates it well above a generic 'husband bad at decorating' joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait. Wait, wait, wait. You said next month.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You specifically said next month.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have... I have a thing next month.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Actually, no. No, I don't. But I could have had a thing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You completely blindsided me into planning a baby shower.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, I mean, somebody like you, or you.' — Jocelyn's barely-concealed manipulation of David into volunteering
jokes.setup: Jocelyn praises 'someone like David' who could help organize the shower, seemingly as a compliment
jokes.punchline: She then points directly at David making it explicit she's been fishing for a volunteer — the misdirection collapses into obvious manipulation
jokes.explanation: A well-structured comedic moment. The 'somebody like you, or you' beat works because Jocelyn drops the social pretense mid-sentence. Good structure and character work showing Jocelyn's desperation overtaking her politeness.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We could do like a 50% discount for people dining alone during Singles Week.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: But if they're dining alone, they're already single.

jokes.text: 'What just happened?'
jokes.setup: David has just been bamboozled into throwing a last-minute baby shower tonight, at someone else's house, for free
jokes.punchline: Patrick's 'What just happened?' perfectly summarizes the audience's bemused reaction and the absurdity of what just transpired
jokes.explanation: A clean button on the opening scene. Patrick as the rational straight-man voicing what the audience feels. Not a high-craft joke but functionally effective as a scene closer.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: What if we offered a discount for single people?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that is genius. We should offer a discount for single people.

jokes.text: Alexis's 'Lonely Hearts Special' — 50% discount for singles dining alone
jokes.setup: Alexis is pitching Singles Week promotional ideas
jokes.punchline: Her idea is to give a discount to people dining alone — which actively rewards isolation rather than encouraging connection, the opposite of the event's goal
jokes.explanation: A good Alexis joke — she presents backwards logic with complete confidence. The comedic engine is that her idea is well-intentioned but structurally self-defeating. Good character integration showing her creative-but-unexamined thinking style.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, hi! What are you doing here?

dialogue.speaker: Klaire
dialogue.line: We came here because it was listed as one of the most random cities.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Random? Like... for fun?

dialogue.speaker: Klaire
dialogue.line: Yeah, we thought it would be hilarious.

jokes.text: Ted gently walking Alexis to the correct idea: discount for two singles eating together
jokes.setup: Ted diplomatically points out the flaw in Alexis's discount logic
jokes.punchline: Alexis then pitches back 'A 2-for-1 discount for 2 singles eating together' as if it's a new breakthrough idea — it's exactly what Ted just suggested
jokes.explanation: A gentle but effective character comedy beat. Alexis's obliviousness — absorbing Ted's correction and repackaging it as her own idea — is very in-character. Ted's cheerful 'That's it, that's the winner!' adds a sweet layer.

dialogue.speaker: Klaire
dialogue.line: Wait, you live here?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, I live here.

dialogue.speaker: Klaire
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I completely forgot about you.

jokes.text: Klaire's friends drove to the 'most random cities in North America' for a Vice guide trip — and ended up in Schitt's Creek
jokes.setup: Klaire explains why she and her friends are in town
jokes.punchline: A Vice guide to 'most random cities' led them here — a meta joke about the town's objectively absurd status as a destination, framed as a hip editorial decision
jokes.explanation: A light meta joke about the town's randomness, filtered through the lens of irony-poisoned city people who think doing something random is inherently cool. Functional but not especially sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Claire
dialogue.line: You look amazing! You look exactly like a woman who woke up this morning and put on clothes!

jokes.text: 'I'm so sorry I didn't put that together, it's like I completely forgot about you.'
jokes.setup: Klaire realizes she's in Alexis's hometown — the town Alexis told her about
jokes.punchline: Her apology for not making the connection is that she 'completely forgot about' Alexis — a casually devastating social dismissal delivered without malice
jokes.explanation: One of the sharper cringe moments in the episode. 'I completely forgot about you' is delivered as an apology, which makes it worse. The oblivious cruelty of Klaire's social world is crystallized here. Good economy and character work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira Rose
dialogue.line: I'll have a water, but I need it to be hot.

dialogue.speaker: Café Worker
dialogue.line: Like tea?

dialogue.speaker: Moira Rose
dialogue.line: No, just hot water. But by the time you bring it to me, it needs to be cool.

jokes.text: 'I'm obsessed with your look. You look just like a small-town waitress! Beep!'
jokes.setup: Alexis is working as a waitress; Klaire is from Alexis's glamorous former world
jokes.punchline: Klaire compliments Alexis's appearance as if 'small-town waitress' is a chic aesthetic choice, punctuated by a perky 'Beep!' — completely unaware this describes Alexis's actual circumstances
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe comedy. Klaire mistakes Alexis's real life for a fashion statement — the oblivious compliment lands as a devastating insult. The 'Beep!' is a perfect finishing touch, a vocal affectation from a world Alexis used to belong to. High quotability because of its specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm having some very dark thoughts right now.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I can't find my facial steamer.

jokes.text: Klaire orders 'a hot water, but if you could just let it sit so it cools — I just need to know it was once hot'
jokes.setup: Klaire is ordering from waitress Alexis
jokes.punchline: Her order is existentially pointless — she wants lukewarm water but requires its hot origin to be verified, like a philosophical stance on hydration
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. 'I just need to know it was once hot' is a brilliant encapsulation of vapid wellness culture — the performance of healthy habits matters more than the outcome. The absurd specificity ('let it sit so it cools') paired with the philosophical justification is exceptional writing. High originality, highly quotable, and deeply character-specific to a certain type of urban lifestyle person.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, here's your hot water that cooled down.

dialogue.speaker: Klaire
dialogue.line: I didn't order that.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You literally just ordered it. You said 'I want hot water that's cooled down to exactly room temperature.'

dialogue.speaker: Klaire
dialogue.line: I would never say that.

jokes.text: Okay. We really have to get you outta here.
jokes.setup: Alexis has just listed her genuine accomplishments with quiet pride.
jokes.punchline: Klaire's response treats everything Alexis said as confirmation she's trapped and needs rescuing, completely missing that Alexis has grown.
jokes.explanation: Klaire's total misreading of Alexis's growth is the thematic engine of this subplot, and this line crystallizes it efficiently. Good economy and character work.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, like, she literally just left. Like, she was just here and then she literally just left. I don't understand why she would just leave like that. Like, literally.

jokes.text: 'Albany literally makes me wanna kill myself.' / 'I'm her best friend.'
jokes.setup: Klaire is venting about Albany, one of her travel companions
jokes.punchline: She says Albany 'makes me wanna kill myself' then immediately adds 'I'm her best friend' — the friendship defined entirely by contempt
jokes.explanation: A sharp little two-liner. The structural joke is the non-sequitur: we expect context or justification after a dramatic statement, and instead we get 'I'm her best friend' as the punchline. Characterizes this entire social world efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What does MADLY stand for?

dialogue.speaker: Ricki
dialogue.line: Moira's Artisanal Dress... Linen... Yarn?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That doesn't spell MADLY.

dialogue.speaker: Ricki
dialogue.line: Well, it sounds better than what it actually stands for.

jokes.text: Klaire waves away the not-so-hot water she ordered: 'Sweetie, no, I didn't order that.'
jokes.setup: Alexis delivers the water Klaire ordered (hot water left to cool)
jokes.punchline: Klaire has completely forgotten the order she just placed and rejects it
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback that doubles as character indictment. The joke isn't just that she forgot — it's that her order was so performative and disconnected from actual desire that she can't even remember placing it. Works beautifully as a two-beat with the original joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We'd like to observe the dating event.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, we're just going to watch from the sidelines.

dialogue.speaker: Ricki
dialogue.line: Oh, so you're group voyeurs? That'll be an extra fifty dollars each.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We're not voyeurs, we're just—

dialogue.speaker: Ricki
dialogue.line: Seventy-five. Final offer.

jokes.text: See you tonight, babe. See you tonight, babe. Hm. Hm. Albany, I was sitting there!
jokes.setup: Klaire leaves after the water incident; Alexis is left processing the interaction.
jokes.punchline: Alexis apparently mimics/echoes Klaire's farewell to herself in a daze, then snaps to awareness when Albany takes her seat — showing how destabilizing this encounter was.
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'See you tonight, babe' in a daze followed by the Albany seat-stealing gag is a well-constructed visual-plus-verbal moment. The final 'Albany, I was sitting there!' is a nice surprise button.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're already matched.

dialogue.speaker: Ricki
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, you'd be surprised. I've seen married couples who aren't compatible.

jokes.text: Johnny's casual framing of Moira being left with 'eligible bachelors' — and his denial that he's concerned
jokes.setup: Johnny explains he came with Moira to observe the singles event
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately calls out that his presence has 'nothing to do with any concerns' about her being surrounded by bachelors — a transparent denial from Johnny
jokes.explanation: A functional marital dynamic joke. Johnny's transparent jealousy dressed as indifference is a reliable comedic engine for this couple, though it's not the sharpest execution of it.

dialogue.speaker: Ricki
dialogue.line: Can I interest you in a little role play?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Ricki
dialogue.line: You didn't even hear what it was.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't need to. The answer is no. However, I will allow you to reorganize my closet by color and then by sleeve length.

jokes.text: But if you ever unleash your feminine wiles on those bachelors, they wouldn't know what hit them.
jokes.setup: Johnny denies jealousy and says Moira can handle herself.
jokes.punchline: He then immediately reveals the jealousy with a charmingly possessive compliment — 'they wouldn't know what hit them' — delivered as flattery but functioning as a warning.
jokes.explanation: A sweet Johnny-Moira beat. His contradiction of himself in two consecutive sentences is a reliable comedic structure, and the 'feminine wiles' phrasing is delightfully old-fashioned and Johnny-specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I felt bad for her. She looked like Adam Sandler on a red carpet—uncomfortable, resentful, and like she'd rather be literally anywhere else.

jokes.text: MADLY stands for 'Mature Adults Dating Lovers, Yes!'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks what MADLY stands for
jokes.punchline: The acronym is revealed to be 'Mature Adults Dating Lovers, Yes!' — the 'Yes!' at the end is not a natural acronym component; it's just there to complete the word, with an enthusiasm that makes it worse
jokes.explanation: A great joke with high craft. The comedy comes from the 'Yes!' — whoever named this organization needed the Y and just appended an affirmation. Ricki's proud delivery of it adds to the absurdity. Memorable, quotable, and a sharp piece of world-building comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I... I didn't realize at the time that...

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Ah!

jokes.text: Ricki confidently announcing she can find 'superlative matches for both of you' — to a married couple
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira have come as observers/auditors, not participants
jokes.punchline: Ricki assumes they're participants and tells this long-married couple she's confident she can find them superlative matches
jokes.explanation: A setup joke that works structurally — the dramatic irony of a matchmaker not recognizing a couple is a solid premise. Reasonably well-executed though not especially surprising.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A sprinkle? What is a sprinkle?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's like a baby shower, but smaller.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So it's a baby drizzle? That's not a thing!

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's actually pretty common for second babies.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it shouldn't be! If you're having a baby, you have a shower. Those are the rules.

jokes.text: 'Well, we could peek in on the experience like group voyeurs, but it would be much more educational for both of you if you would let me immerse you in it.'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to clarify they want to observe, not participate
jokes.punchline: Ricki uses the word 'voyeurs' to describe passive observation and then counters with 'immerse' — the language is slightly too intimate for a business meeting
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe comedy from Ricki's inappropriately intimate vocabulary in a business context. The word 'voyeurs' is the highlight. Functional but not a standout.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A baby sprinkle? What is that? Some sort of... celebratory precipitation?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because if anyone in this family is going to be subjected to sprinkles, it's going to be on my terms, in a controlled environment, with proper dessert napkins.

jokes.text: The Lock and Key activity explained: ladies get keys, men get locks, mingle for an hour to find the 'perfect fit'
jokes.setup: Ricki announces the day's activity
jokes.punchline: The 'Lock and Key' event is presented with complete earnestness, but the sexual metaphor is impossible to ignore — Ricki seems entirely unaware of it
jokes.explanation: The unintentional sexual double entendre of a 'lock and key' event is the joke. Works on a basic level — the straight-faced delivery of an inherently suggestive premise — but isn't the most sophisticated version of this type of joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I mean, we've already gone to great lengths to accommodate her pregnancy. She's been given a comfortable chair, there's a foot stool, we've adjusted the temperature in the room twice. And now she wants us to actually center the party around her?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, it's her baby shower.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, I'm aware. Which is precisely why I've been so thoughtful about her comfort.

jokes.text: 'Well, lucky for us, this lock has already found its key.' / 'You'd be surprised.' / 'Would I?'
jokes.setup: Johnny uses the lock/key metaphor to assert his and Moira's commitment
jokes.punchline: Ricki's 'You'd be surprised' implies she's seen committed couples drift — and Moira's loaded 'Would I?' suggests she too harbors doubts about Johnny's certainty
jokes.explanation: A nice three-way beat with layered irony. Ricki's professional skepticism, Moira's pointed question — the joke works because Moira's 'Would I?' is ambiguous: is she questioning Ricki's surprise, or gently poking at Johnny? Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't understand why we have to accommodate every single person at this event. We have a budget, we have a timeline, and we have very specific aesthetic requirements.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And now I'm being told that we need to have a quiet room for nursing mothers and their infants? A *quiet room*? Do you know what babies do? They cry. Incessantly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So essentially, I'm being sabotaged by people who can't even speak or control their own bodily functions. This is what we've come to.

jokes.text: You'd be surprised. / Would I?
jokes.setup: Johnny confidently declares their relationship is solid.
jokes.punchline: Ricki's 'you'd be surprised' implies the activity might reveal cracks; Johnny's 'Would I?' is a perfectly delivered push-back that's confident but not quite as certain as he pretends.
jokes.explanation: A two-line exchange with nice economy. Ricki plants a seed and Johnny's reply has a beat of genuine doubt under the confidence. Functional and tight.

dialogue.speaker: Claire
dialogue.line: So my stepmom is actually younger than me.

dialogue.speaker: Claire
dialogue.line: And yes, we live together. It's completely normal.

jokes.text: 'Will you grant me access to your most intimate sounds?' / 'No. But we'll play the game.'
jokes.setup: Ricki asks if she can access their 'most intimate sounds' — presumably for some wellness/meditation exercise
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flat 'No' followed by the quick pivot 'But we'll play the game' is a beautifully deadpan shutdown
jokes.explanation: Excellent comedic economy. The request is wonderfully vague and faintly transgressive; Johnny's immediate 'No' is a firm boundary, but 'But we'll play the game' shows his pragmatism. The rhythm of the exchange is perfectly calibrated — one extra word would ruin it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Albany, you're fired.

jokes.text: David describing Jocelyn as looking 'like Adam Sandler on a red carpet'
jokes.setup: David explains to Patrick why he agreed to throw the baby shower
jokes.punchline: His description of Jocelyn's disheveled state is 'looking like Adam Sandler on a red carpet' — a specific, vivid simile for intentionally low-effort presentation
jokes.explanation: A genuinely funny, highly quotable simile. Adam Sandler's deliberate red carpet casualness is a perfect reference — specific enough to be surprising, universally understood, and visually exact for someone in pajamas at a store. High originality in the specificity of the reference. One of the episode's most memorable lines.

dialogue.speaker: Man
dialogue.line: My wife, before she passed, she predicted I would meet someone like you.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, that's so sweet. What did she say I'd be like?

dialogue.speaker: Man
dialogue.line: She said you'd have two arms, two legs, and a vagina.

jokes.text: Branzino express-shipped for a pregnant woman's baby shower
jokes.setup: David is listing his plans for the shower — cheeses, wine, and food
jokes.punchline: He mentions 'Colleen's express-shipping a branzino' — a raw fish is being rush-ordered for a pregnant woman, who cannot eat raw fish
jokes.explanation: A good character joke — David's planning the shower around his own aesthetic rather than the guest of honor. The branzino is both an absurd choice (express-shipped fish?) and practically wrong (raw fish for a pregnant woman). Patrick's callback to it ('booze and fish, not ideal') is well-telegraphed.

dialogue.speaker: Charlene
dialogue.line: You know, Moira, I've always thought you were... special.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh! Well, that's very kind of you to say.

dialogue.speaker: Charlene
dialogue.line: I mean, really special. I think about you all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: So, Heather's supplying some of her cheeses, not at cost, but she's giving us a deal, and I thought we could bring some wine from the store, and Colleen's express-shipping a branzino, so.
jokes.setup: David is planning a baby shower for a small-town resident
jokes.punchline: Express-shipping branzino for a small-town baby shower is so aggressively out of place that it lands as pure character comedy — David cannot help but apply his big-city aesthetic to a Schitt's Creek event
jokes.explanation: The branzino is a great detail — fish typically associated with upscale Manhattan restaurants, express-shipped for a baby sprinkle in rural Canada. Pure David Rose character comedy. The escalating list ('cheeses... wine... branzino') builds nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I tried to jumpstart the holiday spirit with some decorations.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I hung some garland, put up a few lights...

jokes.text: 'Sounding a bit expensive, David.' / 'We haven't talked budget yet, but I'm assuming Jocelyn's wanting to spend some money.' / 'I'm assuming Jocelyn's planning on spending no money.'
jokes.setup: David describes his elaborate shower plans
jokes.punchline: Patrick correctly predicts Jocelyn expects this to be free; David had assumed the opposite with zero basis
jokes.explanation: A clean comedic beat built on class obliviousness — David's default assumption is that people spend money because he always has. Patrick's reality check is instant and accurate. Good structure and character work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I think you should move them somewhere else. Preferably far away. Ideally, a different town.

jokes.text: 'This isn't even her first baby! / Oh, so it's more of a sprinkle, then.' / 'What the hell is a sprinkle?'
jokes.setup: David complains that Jocelyn is having a second baby and shouldn't need a full shower
jokes.punchline: Patrick introduces the concept of a 'sprinkle' (a scaled-down shower for a second child), which David has never heard of and rejects with 'that is the stupidest (bleep) thing I have ever heard'
jokes.explanation: The episode's title joke lands well. The comedic rhythm of Patrick casually introducing 'sprinkle' and David's explosive reaction is perfectly timed. David's outrage at a perfectly reasonable concept is very true to character. Also works as the episode's thematic anchor.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Is that a piñata?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Sure is! Got it at the dollar store. Only cost me a buck.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a baby.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's right. Realistic, too. See how it's all wrinkled and angry-looking? That's what babies look like.

jokes.text: I'm assuming that Jocelyn's planning on spending no money on her baby shower.
jokes.setup: David assumes Jocelyn wants to spend money because she asked him to throw the shower.
jokes.punchline: Patrick corrects the assumption: the person being showered typically pays nothing — David volunteered to foot the bill without realizing it.
jokes.explanation: The dawning horror for David — that he's been talked into not just the labor but the expense — is a good comedic escalation of the opening scene. Patrick's delivery of this information with calm clarity makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't think we should hit the baby with a stick.

jokes.text: 'Well, she's one person, and everyone knows you don't plan a whole party around one person.' / 'Maybe you do, if the party is for her.'
jokes.setup: David defends serving booze and fish at a pregnant woman's baby shower
jokes.punchline: His logic is that you shouldn't plan around one person — Patrick's 'Maybe you do, if the party is for her' is an almost clinical refutation
jokes.explanation: Patrick's response is devastatingly simple and correct. The joke is built on David's genuinely absurd logic being exposed by the most obvious possible counter. Very economical and well-structured. A strong Patrick moment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, no, no. Absolutely not. We are not doing a diaper chocolate game.

jokes.text: 'Says who?' — David's response to the reasonable observation that the party should be for Jocelyn
jokes.setup: Patrick has just made the inarguable point that a party for Jocelyn should be planned around Jocelyn
jokes.punchline: David's 'Says who?' is a beat of pure petulant disbelief — he genuinely can't concede the point
jokes.explanation: Two words and maximum character. 'Says who?' against an obviously true statement is perfectly David — stubborn, privileged, and unwilling to cede aesthetic control. The pause before it (implied by the timestamp gap) amplifies the comedic effect.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: It's a really fun game! You take a diaper, and you melt a chocolate bar inside it, and then everyone has to guess what kind of chocolate it is by smell and taste!

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Wait... oh my God. Oh my God, no. I just realized what that sounds like.

jokes.text: 'Fine! Worst case scenario, she grazes at the soft cheese station.' / 'Are you sure that she can eat that?' / 'This is why I hate babies!'
jokes.setup: David reluctantly concedes but pivots to 'worst case scenario' thinking about the pregnant guest and the cheese
jokes.punchline: Patrick questions whether soft cheese is even safe for pregnancy, prompting David to explode with 'This is why I hate babies!'
jokes.explanation: The escalation lands perfectly. David's 'This is why I hate babies!' is one of the episode's best lines — it's absurd (the babies didn't make soft cheese taboo), it's character-perfect (David's frustrations always locate at the wrong target), and it's deeply quotable. The logic chain is beautifully broken: soft cheese → dietary restriction → babies as the enemy. Strong craft across all dimensions.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, don't you think you should change for the party?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I'm not going to change. This is what I'm wearing.

jokes.text: Fine! Worst case scenario, she grazes at the soft cheese station. — Are you sure that she can eat that?
jokes.setup: David is trying to save his menu by finding a role for Jocelyn at her own shower
jokes.punchline: David's condescending offer of 'she grazes at the soft cheese station' — as if Jocelyn is lucky to participate in her own party — is immediately undercut by Patrick's gentle 'Are you sure that she can eat that?' (soft cheeses are also advised against in pregnancy)
jokes.explanation: Another excellent two-beat exchange. 'She grazes at the soft cheese station' is both condescending and funny — David relegating the guest of honor to a backup position at her own party. Patrick's follow-up (soft cheese is also not recommended in pregnancy) perfectly lands the escalation. The word 'grazes' is particularly good.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Can we move this vase?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, it's bolted down.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why is it bolted down?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's my father's ashes. We've had three urn thefts in this town.

jokes.text: Alexis's excited 'Hey, pack your bags, bitch! You're getting' out!' after receiving the job offer
jokes.setup: Alexis has just received an unexpected job offer from her old friend
jokes.punchline: Her private celebration is gleeful and profane — 'pack your bags, bitch' addressed to herself is a funny release of suppressed ambition
jokes.explanation: A fun character moment that reveals Alexis's internal conflict. Her excitement is genuine and the self-address 'bitch' is endearingly Alexis. Good energy beat, though not especially crafted as a joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would anyone give a baby a piñata? What am I supposed to do with this? Hit it until candy falls out? That seems deeply inappropriate for an infant.

jokes.text: I just like, I can't talk about it around Albany, because she needs to get fired first, so... basically the job is yours, if you want it.
jokes.setup: Klaire is offering Alexis a job but being secretive about it
jokes.punchline: She explains the secrecy with breezy nonchalance — Albany needs to be fired first — delivered as if scheduling someone's firing is a minor logistical detail
jokes.explanation: The casual treatment of someone's imminent firing as a scheduling matter is well-executed dark comedy. 'She needs to get fired first, so...' is a great line. Klaire is an effectively drawn character.

dialogue.speaker: Rene
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Rene Arcand. I'm a real estate agent, a notary public, and I have my Class 5 driver's license.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's... quite the trifecta.

dialogue.speaker: Rene
dialogue.line: The ladies seem to think so.

jokes.text: Man at speed dating says his late wife 'predicted' he would meet a woman with 'shoulder-length blonde hair, and red lipstick' — describing Moira exactly
jokes.setup: A man approaches Moira at the event and shares a touching story about his late wife's dying wish
jokes.punchline: The 'prediction' is suspiciously specific and obviously tailored as a pickup line — 'Telepathic, was she?' Moira asks, and then '...and tremendously specific!'
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed cringe joke. The 'dying wish' pickup line is a bold move, and Moira's 'Telepathic, was she? And tremendously specific!' dismantles it perfectly. The 'tremendously specific!' beat is the real punchline — she identifies exactly what's wrong with his story.

dialogue.speaker: David Rose
dialogue.line: I can drive at night.

dialogue.speaker: Moira Rose
dialogue.line: Well, that's quite the accomplishment.

dialogue.speaker: David Rose
dialogue.line: I know. Most people can't do it. It's a very specific skill that I happen to possess.

jokes.text: Moira advising the man to 'wait until much later in the relationship before mentioning any of the things you just mentioned to me'
jokes.setup: The man has used a bizarre dead-wife prophecy as an opening line
jokes.punchline: Moira gives him earnest dating advice rather than shutting him down — the advice itself implying 'this is wrong but not hopeless, just badly timed'
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical advice-giving is well-deployed here. The implication that the content isn't the problem, just the timing, is a funny subversion. Moira as reluctant mentor to a cringe suitor is good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think the issue is that this lock is clearly not calibrated for someone of your... stature.

jokes.text: Johnny boasting to Charlene that the motel 'never sold out before I showed up' — with zero subtlety
jokes.setup: Johnny is talking to a woman named Charlene at the speed dating event, using his motel as a conversation topic
jokes.punchline: He pivots from pleasant conversation to an unsolicited motel sales pitch, proudly declaring he turned around a failing property
jokes.explanation: Johnny's accidental (or intentional?) flirtation through motel business pride is a mild character comedy beat. 'Maybe I'll spend the night sometime' lands as a double meaning — somewhat funny but not especially crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.line: Moira, I think this lock game is bringing up some deeper issues for you.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course it is. I'm a woman of considerable depth and nuance.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.line: Perhaps we should explore why you're so resistant to failure.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not resistant to failure. I'm resistant to the implication that I could fail at something as pedestrian as a children's puzzle.

jokes.text: 'Maybe I'll spend the night sometime.' / 'At the motel, I mean.'
jokes.setup: Johnny's motel pitch to Charlene could read as a flirtatious invitation
jokes.punchline: He immediately clarifies 'At the motel, I mean' — revealing he caught himself and is flustered by the double meaning
jokes.explanation: The clarification is funnier than the original line. Johnny's self-consciousness about accidentally flirting is very him — the 'Oh, oh, oh' from Moira off-screen and his panicked backtrack is a nice beat. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've devised a baby shower game where guests must guess which item the expectant mother couldn't possibly need.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The winner receives a gift basket. The loser must contemplate their own mortality and inadequacy as a parent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's called 'Whose Baby Survives the Night?'

jokes.text: Moira calling Johnny away by invoking 'Jocelyn's bebe thing' — as an excuse to leave after the neck-burn incident
jokes.setup: Moira is trying the lock key with Rene; Johnny wants to leave; Moira wants one more go
jokes.punchline: After failing to connect her key with Rene's lock (and getting 'neck burn'), Moira suddenly remembers Jocelyn's event as a convenient exit
jokes.explanation: A mild character beat — Moira saving face by pivoting to an excuse. The humor is in her transparent shift from 'I want to keep trying' to 'we must go.' Functional but not a highlight.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We would have used real drugs if we could afford them.

jokes.text: Roland offering a baby piñata from the dollar store — 'pretty realistic' — that he didn't even pay for
jokes.setup: David and Patrick arrive at Jocelyn's house to find Roland has been 'helping' with decorations
jokes.punchline: Roland reveals a baby-shaped piñata he got free from the dollar store, describing it as 'pretty realistic' — and David can't believe they're being asked to smash a baby with a stick
jokes.explanation: Roland's contributions are consistently the right kind of wrong — the baby piñata is both thematically inappropriate and physically alarming. 'Don't think we can smash a baby with a stick tonight' is a highlight. The 'they just gave it to me' detail adds to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh, I know this game. This is the game where you take pills and see who dies first.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, statistically speaking, it's usually the parent.

jokes.text: 'Don't think we can smash a baby with a stick tonight, but...' / 'It's not a real baby, Dave.'
jokes.setup: Roland has brought a realistic baby piñata
jokes.punchline: David's 'don't think we can smash a baby with a stick tonight' is met with Roland's earnest correction 'It's not a real baby, Dave' — as if that was genuinely in question
jokes.explanation: The David/Roland dynamic at its best — David's horror at the object, Roland's literal-minded defense. The comedy is in the implication that Roland thought clarification was necessary. Good economy and character work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, you were good at it?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was very good at it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You were drugging Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was a child!

jokes.text: David being wary about what will fall out of the dollar-store baby piñata
jokes.setup: Roland mentions the piñata was free from the dollar store
jokes.punchline: David expresses concern about what mystery items a dollar-store baby piñata might contain — a reasonable but darkly funny anxiety
jokes.explanation: A small but good beat. David's snobbery applied to piñata contents is very him. The joke is in the unspoken image of what a dollar-store baby might be filled with.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I appreciate the offer, but I'm going to have to pass on the job.

dialogue.speaker: Klaire
dialogue.line: What job?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: The one you just offered me.

dialogue.speaker: Klaire
dialogue.line: I offered you a job? When?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Like, five minutes ago.

dialogue.speaker: Klaire
dialogue.line: I have no recollection of that whatsoever.

jokes.text: The Diaper Game explanation: melt chocolate into diapers, guests sniff and guess the bar
jokes.setup: Roland enthusiastically mentions 'the diaper game'
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn explains: you melt chocolate bars into diapers and guests guess the bar by sniffing — a classic baby shower game that sounds deeply unpleasant when described literally
jokes.explanation: The diaper game is a real thing, so the joke is observational — the gap between how cheerfully it's described and how objectively disgusting it sounds. Roland's enthusiasm ('Ah, good!') adds to it. Not original but well-deployed.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: We interrupt this program to bring you a special report. Sources close to the Rose family confirm that their hatred of Albany has not subsided. In fact, it appears to be growing stronger by the day.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: When asked for comment, David Rose simply said, 'It's a dump.' Moira Rose was unavailable, as she was busy designing a hat that says 'Albany Stinks.'

jokes.text: 'I will literally do anything for us to not play that game.' — David's response to the diaper game
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is enthusiastically describing the diaper game
jokes.punchline: David's 'I will literally do anything' is both a hyperbolic refusal and a direct motivator — his disgust drives the plot forward
jokes.explanation: A functional character beat — David's aversion to the diaper game leads to the 'Sleepy Mommy' invention. The 'literally do anything' is simultaneously comedy and plot engine. Good economy.

jokes.text: 'You don't just sniff it.' — Jocelyn's correction of David's reductive description of the diaper game
jokes.setup: David dismisses the diaper game as just sniffing a diaper
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's earnest correction implies there is more to it, which only makes it worse — then Patrick seconds with 'No. You don't.'
jokes.explanation: The two-beat exchange is elegant: Jocelyn corrects David, Patrick validates Jocelyn in a way that makes it worse, and nobody explains what else you do. The implication is funnier than any description would be. Good economy.

jokes.text: I will literally do anything for us to not play that game.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has described the diaper game (chocolate melted in diapers, guess the bar)
jokes.punchline: David's absolute commitment — 'literally do ANYTHING' — to avoid the diaper game is both funny and telling, given that David typically doesn't commit to doing anything
jokes.explanation: David's absolute commitment to avoiding a specific baby shower game is funny in its proportion to the threat. The setup makes the 'literally anything' land well. Solid character beat.

jokes.text: Hey, Jocelyn, why don't you get ready, get changed, and we'll take good care of the place. — I am ready.
jokes.setup: Patrick tries to politely get Jocelyn out of the way so they can decorate; he suggests she 'get ready'
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's flat 'I am ready' — she's still in her pajamas from the opening scene — is a perfect callback button
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback to the episode's opening — Jocelyn is STILL in her pajamas. 'I am ready' is the perfect two-word callback punchline. Economy is near-perfect. The rewatch bonus is earned because it rewards viewers who caught the callback.

jokes.text: Jocelyn saying 'I am ready' when asked if she wants to get changed — she's still in her pajamas
jokes.setup: David tries to diplomatically get Jocelyn out of her pajamas by suggesting she 'get ready'
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn, still in her pajamas, announces 'I am ready' — indicating she considers her current state appropriate for her own baby shower
jokes.explanation: Callbacks to the opening scene — she was in pajamas then and she's still in them now. The joke is in the complete sincerity of her delivery and the callback to the Adam Sandler comparison. Good visual comedy beat.

jokes.text: Grandpa's ashes are in a bolted-down urn because of 'urn thefts'
jokes.setup: David asks if a vase is movable for decoration purposes
jokes.punchline: Roland reveals it's his grandfather's ashes, bolted down due to urn theft concerns — a darkly absurd combination of family sentiment and small-town paranoia
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The comedy layers are exceptional: (1) the urn is decorative enough that David can't tell it from a vase, (2) it contains a human being, (3) it's bolted down for theft prevention, (4) 'there's been a lot of urn thefts lately' suggests this is an established local phenomenon. Each detail compounds the absurdity. Roland's nonchalant delivery is essential. Highly quotable and very rewatchable.

jokes.text: 'Was this a mistake?' / 'Oh, probably, but you know, we're here now, you know.'
jokes.setup: David surveys the chaos at Jocelyn's house — bolted urns, baby piñata, Roland with a ratchet
jokes.punchline: Patrick's 'Oh, probably' is a perfect validation of David's worst fears, followed by the resigned 'we're here now' as the only available philosophy
jokes.explanation: A warm, understated exchange. Patrick's honesty ('Oh, probably') is funnier than false reassurance would be, and the resigned optimism of 'we're here now' is very Patrick. Good couple dynamic joke.

jokes.text: David clearly struggling with where to put the baby piñata — visual beat of holding it awkwardly
jokes.setup: Roland has handed David the baby piñata to hold
jokes.punchline: David holds the realistic baby piñata with evident discomfort — no good place to put it, no good way to hold it, and his expression communicates maximum distaste
jokes.explanation: An inferred visual gag. The '(bleep) thing' line and the timestamp gap suggest an awkward physical comedy beat with David holding the piñata. Functional visual comedy but difficult to fully score from transcript alone.

jokes.text: Rene at the speed dating: 'I work in ad sales and can drive at night. I know that sounds like an odd thing to mention, but it does separate me from the crowd.'
jokes.setup: Rene introduces himself to Moira at the lock-and-key event
jokes.punchline: His distinguishing credential among the singles group is that he can drive at night — which he knows is 'an odd thing to mention' but frames as a genuine competitive advantage
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character joke. 'Can drive at night' as a defining personal quality implies the competition is limited to people who cannot — suggesting a pool of elderly or vision-impaired singles. Rene's self-awareness ('I know that sounds like an odd thing to mention') makes it funnier by acknowledging the absurdity without quite resolving it. The deadpan delivery of a profoundly modest achievement as a differentiator is exceptional writing.

jokes.text: Pardonnez-moi, senor Rene. Okay, Moira, let's go.
jokes.setup: Moira is clearly enjoying Rene's company; Johnny appears to pull her away.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'Pardonnez-moi, senor Rene' is a hilariously mixed-language dismissal — 'pardon me' in French and 'sir' in Spanish simultaneously — suggesting flustered jealousy.
jokes.explanation: The mixed French/Spanish address is a tiny perfect detail — Johnny in a jealous rush reaches for formality but grabs the wrong language family. Character-specific and funny without being labored.

jokes.text: Moira's 'neck burn' from trying too hard to connect the key to a lock — and Ricki's passive-aggressive suggestion that she 'go within and reflect'
jokes.setup: Moira has tried multiple locks and is now visibly straining to connect with Rene's lock
jokes.punchline: She's gotten 'neck burn' from the effort, and Ricki — after claiming the locks are fine — suggests Moira reflect on why she's having 'trouble connecting'
jokes.explanation: A great Moira moment. The physical comedy of getting 'neck burn' from a key event is absurd, and Ricki's therapeutic pivot — suggesting Moira examine why she can't connect — is a dig that hits harder because Moira's 'connecting is my livelihood' defense is so wounded. Good character integration and earned comedy.

jokes.text: You're um... getting a bit of neck burn. / Oh. / Well, I didn't have any problem with mine.
jokes.setup: The lock-and-key game has left marks on Moira; Johnny had no trouble.
jokes.punchline: Ricki's professional observation that Moira got 'neck burn' from the lock trying is immediately one-upped by Johnny's pointed 'well, I didn't have any problem with mine' — implying that Moira strained against the lock while he didn't.
jokes.explanation: A functional physical comedy inference — the neck burn implies Moira was really working at those locks. Johnny's smug observation is a petty jealousy joke that works in the context of their sparring.

jokes.text: 'Connecting is my livelihood.' / 'No, it's these discount locks.'
jokes.setup: Ricki suggests Moira reflect on her difficulty 'connecting'
jokes.punchline: Moira defends herself with 'Connecting is my livelihood' — a grand theatrical claim — then immediately blames the equipment ('No, it's these discount locks')
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira moment. Her initial wounded dignity ('Connecting is my livelihood') followed immediately by the petty blame-shift to 'discount locks' is perfectly in character. The comedy comes from the gap between her grandeur and her pettiness. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Johnny offering Rene to drive Shirley home — testing him on his key attribute ('can drive at night')
jokes.setup: Rene introduced himself earlier by noting he 'can drive at night' as a distinguishing feature
jokes.punchline: Johnny calls back to this specific credential at the end, testing whether Rene can live up to his one marketable trait
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed callback. 'Can drive at night' as a singles event credential is funny on its own; Johnny weaponizing it as a practical assignment is a good payoff. Rewards attentive viewers.

jokes.text: Rene, Shirley needs a ride home. Can you still drive at night? / Sure.
jokes.setup: The event is wrapping up; Shirley needs a ride.
jokes.punchline: Johnny asks Rene if he can 'still drive at night' — a callback to Rene's earlier self-introduction — now deployed as a practical utility, reducing Rene's charming opening line to a logistical asset.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully executed callback. Johnny weaponizes Rene's romantic opener as a practical logistics question, simultaneously vindicating Rene's claim that it 'separates him from the crowd' and deflating any romantic threat. The callback pays off the earlier joke while adding a layer of competitive pettiness from Johnny. 'Sure.' as Rene's entire response is perfect.

jokes.text: The 'Sleepy Mommy' game — David's alternative to the diaper game
jokes.setup: David promised to come up with games that don't involve diapers
jokes.punchline: His invention is 'Sleepy Mommy' — guests throw breath mints (which he calls 'tranqs') into Jocelyn's open mouth. He notes 'we couldn't afford actual benzos'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's peak jokes. The escalation from 'no diaper game' to 'drug the pregnant lady' is absurdist perfection, and 'we couldn't afford actual benzos' is a devastating line that implies David would have used real sedatives if budget allowed. The game also works as dark comedy — it's a baby shower game involving sedation. The fact that everyone loves it is the final punchline. Exceptional character integration: only David would invent this.

jokes.text: Okay, now this game is called sleepy mommy... We're each gonna take turns popping pills into mommy's mouth. Now unfortunately, these are breath mints, we couldn't afford actual benzos...
jokes.setup: David has invented an alternative to the diaper game.
jokes.punchline: His replacement game involves throwing pills at a pregnant woman's open mouth — and his apology that they're only breath mints ('couldn't afford actual benzos') implies actual tranquilizers were on the table.
jokes.explanation: The 'sleepy mommy' game is a genuinely inspired piece of dark comedy. David has invented a party game that is objectively worse than the diaper game, presented with complete seriousness. The detail that he 'couldn't afford actual benzos' implies he looked into it. It's original, deeply character-specific (only David would invent this), structurally clean, and highly quotable. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Boy, these have a bit more weight to them than the usual pills.
jokes.setup: Johnny is playing sleepy mommy — trying to pop breath mints into Jocelyn's mouth.
jokes.punchline: His observation about the pills' weight is delivered as genuine feedback, implying he has personal reference data for 'the usual pills' — a darkly funny non-sequitur.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's casual expertise with 'the usual pills' implies he's familiar with the benchmark — the joke is what this reveals about how David's childhood games were run. Works on a dark implication level and is very well-delivered.

jokes.text: Well, you know, Johnny, that weight might actually work in your favour if you take into consideration the humidity in here.
jokes.setup: Johnny notes the breath mints are heavier than expected pills.
jokes.punchline: Roland offers a technical analysis of pill-throwing physics (humidity affecting trajectory) as if this is a serious game with legitimate strategy.
jokes.explanation: Roland treating the pill-tossing game with the strategic seriousness of a sports coach is perfect Roland. 'The humidity in here' as a genuine variable in breath-mint trajectory is absurdist gold. The game context makes this even funnier because everyone is playing along.

jokes.text: 'David, why does this game feel vaguely familiar?' / 'You invented it. We played it a lot growing up.' / 'Oh! / I can't remember playing.' / 'That's because we got very good at it.'
jokes.setup: Moira is playing 'Sleepy Mommy' and recognizes the game
jokes.punchline: David reveals she invented it — and they played it constantly. Moira's inability to remember is because they 'got very good at it' — implying she was regularly sedated
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode and one of the best Rose family dark comedy beats in the series. The revelation recontextualizes everything: the game wasn't invented for this shower — it was a childhood coping mechanism. Moira used to drug herself to get through parenting. David and Alexis playing it so often that they got 'very good at it' implies deep familial dysfunction delivered as a warm memory. The structure is perfect: recognition → reveal → apparent gap → devastating explanation. Each line lands harder than the last. Extremely high craft across all dimensions.

jokes.text: 'I remember Alexis got one in from across the room once.'
jokes.setup: The revelation that David and Alexis used to play Sleepy Mommy on their actual mother
jokes.punchline: David shares this fondly as a childhood achievement — Alexis's trick shot from across the room is offered as a nostalgic highlight
jokes.explanation: An excellent button on the Sleepy Mommy revelation. The warmth and pride in David's voice as he recalls Alexis's distance achievement makes it funnier and darker simultaneously. It confirms this was a regular family activity with technique and progression. Exceptional character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Yeah, we need some clarity on the point system, David, because I've landed three in Jocelyn's bangs, and that's gotta count for something. / Or nothing.
jokes.setup: Johnny has been playing competitively and is lobbying for his bang-shots to count.
jokes.punchline: David's flat 'Or nothing' dismisses the entire hair-landing category.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'landed three in Jocelyn's bangs' is funny on its own; 'or nothing' is a perfect David deadpan. Good economy in the punchline.

jokes.text: Roland asking if he gets an extra point for bouncing one off Jocelyn's forehead — and the scoring debate
jokes.setup: Everyone is enthusiastically playing Sleepy Mommy
jokes.punchline: Roland escalates to trick-shot territory, and Johnny reveals he's already landed three in Jocelyn's bangs and demands those count for something
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed escalation of the Sleepy Mommy sequence. The comedy is in how quickly and completely everyone has bought into this game — Johnny's competitive scoring grievance about bangs-landings is a great detail. Shows how the whole community adapts to David's bizarre vision.

jokes.text: Klaire's Cabo/Mexico/Tulum dismissal — 'what does she wanna do, smoke a blunt with Pitbull?'
jokes.setup: Klaire is complaining about another friend going to Cabo instead of Tulum
jokes.punchline: She dismisses Cabo with 'what does she wanna do, smoke a blunt with Pitbull?' — a specific, funny image of Cabo's celebrity and party reputation
jokes.explanation: A fun, specific pop-culture reference that works because of its precision. Pitbull as the avatar of Cabo tourism is accurate and funny — and Klaire delivers it as a genuine criticism rather than a fun option. Strong character integration for Klaire's snobbishness.

jokes.text: So like, she's telling me that she's going to Mexico, and I'm like, if you're not going to Tulum, then like where the hell are you even going? / So where is she going? / Ugh, god. Cabo. / Oh my god, poor thing.
jokes.setup: Klaire is sharing a story about a friend's vacation plans.
jokes.punchline: The dramatic buildup to 'Cabo' as the shameful destination, and Alexis's sympathetic 'Oh my god, poor thing' — both characters treating a luxury Mexican resort as a humiliation — is a perfect satire of social hierarchy.
jokes.explanation: The Tulum-vs-Cabo status hierarchy played completely straight is a precise satire of elite social geography. Alexis's reflexive 'oh my god, poor thing' is a character beat that shows she's briefly slipping back into her old values — the joke has some thematic weight too.

jokes.text: Klaire doesn't remember offering Alexis the job — 'I'm sorry, reset. What are we talking about?'
jokes.setup: Alexis has come to give her answer about the job offer Klaire made
jokes.punchline: Klaire has no memory of making the offer — 'I'm sorry, reset. What are we talking about?' — making Alexis's considered decision meaningless
jokes.explanation: An excellent cringe payoff to the job offer setup. Alexis has agonized over this decision, made a meaningful choice to stay, and Klaire has literally forgotten she offered the job. The 'reset' verbal tic perfectly captures Klaire's relationship with other people's emotional weight. Devastating and very funny. Strong character integration for both characters.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, sure, you do your bit. I'm always here for you.
jokes.setup: Alexis declines the job; Klaire says she understands.
jokes.punchline: 'Do your bit' frames Alexis's entire life in Schitt's Creek as a temporary performance piece — a 'bit' she's doing rather than a life she's chosen.
jokes.explanation: 'Do your bit' is an unusually cutting line for what presents as supportive. It encapsulates how Klaire and her world see Alexis's growth — as an eccentric phase. Works on both comedy and thematic levels.

jokes.text: 'Oh, yeah, sure, you do your bit. I'm always here for you.'
jokes.setup: Alexis explains she's decided to stay in Schitt's Creek for now
jokes.punchline: Klaire responds with condescending support — 'do your bit' frames Alexis's life choices as a temporary affectation, not a real decision
jokes.explanation: The condescension in 'do your bit' is perfectly observed — it's the language of someone who views another person's authentic life as a temporary adventure. Good character work for Klaire and painful for Alexis's arc.

jokes.text: Breaking news, we still (bleep) hate her.
jokes.setup: Klaire is talking about Albany, who apparently paid for the drinks.
jokes.punchline: 'Breaking news' as a framing device for delivering gossip about someone sitting nearby is an absurd media parody delivered with complete naturalness.
jokes.explanation: 'Breaking news' as a delivery mechanism for casual social cruelty is a sharp comedic choice. Very Klaire, very quotable.

jokes.text: Alexis's final quiet 'It won't' — committing to Schitt's Creek after Klaire's hollow 'we can't let this happen again'
jokes.setup: Klaire does the performative 'we can't let this happen again' at the end of their reunion
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'It won't' is delivered with quiet certainty — she means it, but not in the way Klaire intends. Alexis is choosing not to let this friendship continue, not to prevent future distance
jokes.explanation: A beautiful piece of comedic understatement with genuine emotional weight. Two words that reverse Klaire's meaning entirely. Alexis has grown — she's no longer chasing this world. The laugh comes from the subversion and the recognition that Alexis means something completely different. Exceptional economy.

jokes.text: 'Why do you guys have drinks, and I don't? Hmm?' — Albany asserting herself at the end
jokes.setup: Albany has been discussed and mocked throughout the episode without ever having a proper moment
jokes.punchline: Her first real line of the episode is a petulant but reasonable complaint about being left out of drinks — a funny punctuation that humanizes her slightly while confirming the group dynamic
jokes.explanation: A good closing button. Albany's entire presence has been discussed in absentia — she's been the villain of Klaire's stories — and her actual appearance is disarmingly normal. The comedy is in the anticlimax: the much-maligned Albany just wants a drink.

jokes.text: Why do you guys have drinks, and I don't? Hmm?
jokes.setup: Albany has been the off-screen villain of the subplot — hated by everyone, about to be fired, unwanted.
jokes.punchline: Albany's only line in the episode is a petulant complaint about drinks — blissfully unaware of her social situation, functioning as a visual gag/reaction beat ending.
jokes.explanation: Albany's first and only appearance lands as the episode's button. She's been built up as a monster and her actual introduction is a completely mundane complaint — the contrast between expectation and reality is the joke. Works as a closing tag.

tmdb_overview: For research, Johnny and Moira attend a singles event but get too involved. Alexis runs into an old friend, David agrees to help Jocelyn in planning her baby shower.
insight: Character comedy drives 84.9-rated episode with 49 jokes across mild cringe moments.
